Article.ArticleTitle: The effect of pain on fear of movement in the early period after open heart surgery according to the fear-avoidance model: the role of moderating factors.

Abstract.AbstractText: Postoperative pain and fear of movement are critical factors that can adversely affect recovery outcomes. The interplay among these factors can significantly influence mobilization and rehabilitation outcomes. This study aims to examine the correlation between pain and fear of mobility during the initial phase following open-heart surgery, taking into account the influence of moderating variables.

This cross-sectional and correlational study, conducted within the framework of the Fear-Avoidance Model, involved 203 patients in a Cardiovascular Surgery Intensive Care Unit. Data were collected using a "Patient Information Form," the "Numeric Rating Scale" and the "Tampa Scale of Kinesiophobia." These data collection tools were administered to patients at specific time points (12, 24, and 36 hours). The data collection process lasted approximately 30 minutes for each patient.

A weak, positive connection was identified between pain and fear of movement at 12 h post-surgery. A comparable correlation was discovered between pre-mobilization discomfort and mobility apprehension at the 24-hour mark. At 24 h post-mobilization, the association between pain and fear of movement shifted to a moderate, negative correlation after mobilization. All demographic and health-related variables, with the exception of body mass index (BMI), were determined to have no moderating influence on the link between pain and fear of mobility. At 24 h post-surgery, post-mobilization BMI influenced the correlation between pain and fear of mobility. In patients with a lower BMI, the fear of mobility diminished as pain intensified. The identical relationship was noted prior to mobilization at 36 h following surgery.

This study demonstrates that the direction and strength of the correlation between pain and fear of movement vary across the early postoperative period, with body mass index functioning as a significant moderating variable. The significance of pain management and early mobilization in the postoperative phase is emphasized. Thus, personalized patient management planning by nurses and healthcare experts is essential for improving patient comfort and facilitating the recovery process.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Ethics approval and consent to participate: Written permissions were obtained from the Cukurova University Faculty of Medicine Non-Interventional Clinical Research Ethics Committee (Meeting Number/Decision Number/Date: 145/71/14.06.2024) and the Health Sciences University Adana City Training and Research Hospital Chief Physician. Before starting the research, the purpose of the study was explained to the participants and both verbal and written consents were obtained. For the reliability of the study, care was taken to ensure that the participants volunteered to participate in the study. It was stated that the participants had to volunteer for the reliability of the research results, that the information given to the questions was accurate and that it was very important for the reliability of the research results, that the information received would only be used for this study, that the personal information received would not be shared with other persons or institutions in any way, that the data would be kept confidential, and that no disruption would occur in the medical treatment in line with the data received. In this context, the principles of “Informed Consent”, “Respect for Autonomy”, “Confidentiality and Protection of Confidentiality” were fulfilled. In addition, the research was conducted in accordance with the ethical principles stated in the World Medical Association Declaration of Helsinki. Consent for publication: Not applicable. Competing interests: The authors declare no competing interests.
